free_text: Alpha-decay of excited states in C-13 and C-14.
School of Physics and Astronomy
C-14 foil, thickness 280-mug/cm**2, 80% enrichment,
was used.
Two forward mounted telescopes
each consisting of three elements-
The first elements  were silicon detectors with an area
of 50*50-mm**2 and thickness of 70 mum.
The second elements were position-sensitive silicon
strip detectors with an area of 50*50 mm**2 and a
thickness of 500 mum.
The final elements of the telescopes were 1-cm thick
Cs(I)-scintillation detectors.
*By authors*.Analysis was performed on the break-up of
the two nuclei C-13 and C-14 into Be-9+a and Be-10+a,
respectively.
The experimental excitation energy spectra normalized
for the detection efficiency.
The Vivitron accelerator facility,
Strasbourg, France.
coincident detection of Be-9 nuclei and alpha-
particle and Be-10 and alpha-particles.
The uncertainty is reported by authors.
.Data were received from Neil Curtis.
.By N.Curtis 03.06.2008 without remarks.
S.B
Figure 5
*By authors*.The angular distributions of the C-13
nuclei produced in the C-14(C-13,C-13-exc)C-14 reaction
can be calculated via the momenta of the decay
fragments.
The plots have been normalized for the variation in
detection efficiency as a function of the centre-of-
mass scattering angle as predicted by Monte Carlo
simulations(note that the data on figure have been
scaled so that their differential cross sections can
be shown in a single figure and thus the units are
arbitrary.
Figure 6
See comments.
*By authors*.The results of the coupled reaction
channels analysis are shown in fig.6. In the same
measurements singles C-14(C-13,C-13) scattering data
were recorded in addition to coincidence break-up
data.This permitted the extraction of the C-13+C-14
elastic and inelastic scattering yields.
